    issue_reg = ir.async_get(hass)
    issue_id = f"lock_setup_failed_{lock.lock.entity_id}"

    no_pin_caps = LockCapabilities(
        supports_user_management=True, max_users=10, credential_types={}
    )
    caps_mock = AsyncMock(return_value=no_pin_caps)
    with patch.object(lock, "async_get_capabilities", caps_mock):
        await lock.async_setup_internal(lock.lock_config_entry)

        issue = issue_reg.async_get_issue(DOMAIN, issue_id)
        assert issue is not None
        assert issue.translation_placeholders
        assert "PIN credential" in issue.translation_placeholders["error"]

        # The lock's condition is fixed; the LOADED transition revalidates
        # and the repair dismisses itself.
        caps_mock.return_value = _pin_capabilities()
        loaded_entry = MagicMock()
        loaded_entry.state = ConfigEntryState.LOADED
        lock.lock_config_entry = loaded_entry
        await lock._async_on_integration_loaded()

    assert issue_reg.async_get_issue(DOMAIN, issue_id) is None

    await lock.coordinator.async_shutdown()
    await lock.async_unload(False)


async def test_reconnect_validation_failure_raises_repair_issue(
    hass: HomeAssistant,
):
    """Deferred validation failing on the reconnect path raises the repair too."""
    lock = _make_base_test_lock(
        hass, "test_lock_repair_issue_reconnect", MockNativeUserLock
    )
    lock.set_connected(False)
    issue_reg = ir.async_get(hass)
    issue_id = f"lock_setup_failed_{lock.lock.entity_id}"

    no_pin_caps = LockCapabilities(
        supports_user_management=True, max_users=10, credential_types={}
    )
    caps_mock = AsyncMock(return_value=no_pin_caps)
    with patch.object(lock, "async_get_capabilities", caps_mock):
        await lock.async_setup_internal(lock.lock_config_entry)
        # Deferred: no probe ran, so no issue yet.
        assert issue_reg.async_get_issue(DOMAIN, issue_id) is None

        lock.set_connected(True)
        loaded_entry = MagicMock()
        loaded_entry.state = ConfigEntryState.LOADED
        lock.lock_config_entry = loaded_entry
        await lock._async_on_integration_loaded()

    assert issue_reg.async_get_issue(DOMAIN, issue_id) is not None

    await lock.coordinator.async_shutdown()
    await lock.async_unload(False)


async def test_unload_permanently_clears_setup_failed_repair_issue(
    hass: HomeAssistant,
):
    """Removing a lock permanently clears its setup-failed repair issue.

    A non-permanent unload (reload, HA restart) must keep the persistent
    issue so it survives until the failure actually resolves.
    """
    lock = _make_base_test_lock(hass, "test_lock_repair_unload", MockNativeUserLock)
    issue_reg = ir.async_get(hass)
    issue_id = f"lock_setup_failed_{lock.lock.entity_id}"

    no_pin_caps = LockCapabilities(
        supports_user_management=True, max_users=10, credential_types={}
    )
    with patch.object(
        lock, "async_get_capabilities", AsyncMock(return_value=no_pin_caps)
    ):
        await lock.async_setup_internal(lock.lock_config_entry)
    assert issue_reg.async_get_issue(DOMAIN, issue_id) is not None

    await lock.async_unload(False)
    assert issue_reg.async_get_issue(DOMAIN, issue_id) is not None

    await lock.async_unload(True)
    assert issue_reg.async_get_issue(DOMAIN, issue_id) is None

    await lock.coordinator.async_shutdown()

content.subject: PR A fix 2: one-shot getUsersCount recovery before concluding zero PIN slots
content.description: In zwave_js.async_get_capabilities, when caps are degenerate (no PIN type or num_slots==0) and node advertises User Code CC, invoke UC CC API getUsersCount once (persists supportedUsers) and re-read capabilities before raising. TDD.

content.subject: PR A fix 3: keep structurally-failed locks degraded instead of dropping
content.description: async_setup_internal should treat LockCodeManagerProviderError like the reconnect path: log error, keep lock in runtime_data with coordinator/entities, revalidate on integration reload. Update tests asserting drop behavior.

content.subject: PR A fix 4: merge options in data→options migration instead of clobbering
content.description: _setup_entry_after_start overwrites options with stale data when both are non-empty (options saved while entry in SETUP_ERROR get discarded). Merge options-preferred. Regression test.

        """Tear down config-entry-state listener, reconnect task, and push subscription."""

python3 - <<'EOF'
import json

msg_title = "Lock setup failed: {lock_entity_id}"
msg_desc = (
    "Lock Code Manager could not set up `{lock_entity_id}`: {error}\n\n"
    "Its entities are created but unavailable, and code synchronization is "
    "paused for this lock. After addressing the cause, reload the lock's "
    "integration (or Lock Code Manager) to retry. This issue will be "
    "automatically dismissed when setup succeeds."
)

for path in (
    "/Users/raman/projects/lock_code_manager/custom_components/lock_code_manager/strings.json",
    "/Users/raman/projects/lock_code_manager/custom_components/lock_code_manager/translations/en.json",
):
    with open(path) as f:
        data = json.load(f)
    issues = data["issues"]
    # insert after lock_offline preserving order
    new_issues = {}
    for k, v in issues.items():
        new_issues[k] = v
        if k == "lock_offline":
            new_issues["lock_setup_failed"] = {"title": msg_title, "description": msg_desc}
    data["issues"] = new_issues
    with open(path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(data, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)
        f.write("\n")
    print("updated", path)
EOF

feat: surface structural lock setup failures as a repair issue

input.command:
gh pr create --base fix/zwave-caps-recovery-and-degraded-locks --title "feat: surface structural lock setup failures as a repair issue" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Proposed change

A lock that fails structural validation (no PIN credential support, zero usable slots) reports its actionable remedy — e.g. zwave_js's "re-interview the lock with it awake and update Z-Wave JS" — only in the log, addressed to a user who never sees it. From the UI the lock just looks like LCM "won't accept it" ([community forum report](https://community.home-assistant.io/t/695681/86)).

This raises a persistent, non-fixable `lock_setup_failed` repair issue carrying the provider's actionable error message. Lifecycle:

- **Raised** when structural validation fails, on both the initial-setup path and the integration-reconnect path.
- **Dismissed automatically** when revalidation succeeds on the provider integration's LOADED transition, when the lock is permanently removed from the entry, and on entry removal (gated on no other entry managing the lock, matching `lock_offline`).

**Stacked on #1344** — that PR keeps structurally-failed locks degraded instead of dropping them, which is what makes the failure observable and recoverable enough to anchor a repair to. This PR retargets to `main` automatically once #1344 merges.

## Type of change

- [ ] Dependency upgrade
- [ ] Bugfix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [x] New feature (which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix/feature causing existing functionality to break)
- [ ] Code quality improvements to existing code or addition of tests

## Additional information

- This PR is related to issue: #1298 and PR #1344

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -1

content.content: Updated task #5 status

content.text:
Both PRs are up:

**PR A — [#1344](https://github.com/raman325/lock_code_manager/pull/1344)** `fix/zwave-caps-recovery-and-degraded-locks` (fixes 2, 3, 4):

- **Fix 2 (zwave_js recovery):** When the capability probe reports a PIN type with zero slots and the node advertises User Code CC, the provider now invokes the CC API `getUsersCount` once and re-reads capabilities before raising. This was the one non-obvious design point: tracing node-zwave-js showed `refreshValues` reads `supportedUsers` from the same cache that's missing (`?? 0`), so `async_refresh_cc_values` can't repair this state — only the `getUsersCount` device query persists the solicited UsersNumberReport back into the value DB.
- **Fix 3 (no more silent drops):** Structural validation failures during initial setup now degrade the lock exactly like the reconnect path — coordinator, entities, and the recovery listener are created, and the LOADED transition revalidates. Also fixed a latent trap the new test exposed: a degenerate-but-successful capability read was being cached before validation raised, which would have made recovery impossible; validation failure now invalidates the cache. Contract tests in matter/seam/base that pinned the old raise behavior were updated.
- **Fix 4 (options clobber):** The data→options migration now merges options-preferred, so an options-flow save made while the entry was in SETUP_ERROR (exactly Craig's post-exclusion window) survives the next setup instead of being silently discarded.

**PR B — [#1345](https://github.com/raman325/lock_code_manager/pull/1345)** `fix/lock-setup-failed-repair` (fix 1, stacked on #1344): raises a persistent `lock_setup_failed` repair carrying the provider's actionable error text; auto-dismissed when revalidation succeeds, when the lock is permanently removed, and on entry removal (shared-lock gated, matching `lock_offline`). Strings + en translations added. It targets #1344's branch and will retarget to `main` when that merges.

Verification: full suite passed on both branches (1266 and 1269 passed respectively), `prek` clean on both. Commits follow the no-amend/commit-forward convention.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
The three fixes compose into a coherent story for Craig's exact sequence: the re-included lock now self-heals at setup (fix 2); if it still can't, it stays visible and auto-recovers after a re-interview + integration reload instead of vanishing (fix 3); the repair issue tells him what to do (PR B); and any config change he saves while the entry is down is no longer eaten (fix 4).
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

Remaining loose end: the reply to Craig on the forum thread. Even with these fixes shipping, his sibling lock's root state is still unconfirmed — want me to draft a response asking for the discriminating evidence (log lines for `Failed to set up lock` / `no usable PIN slots`, zwave-js-ui driver version, and whether CC 99 values exist on the two unmanaged nodes), and mentioning the upcoming release?

content.text: I'll use the code-review skill for this.

content.content: Launching skill: code-review

content.text:
`high effort → 3+5 angles × 6 candidates → 1-vote verify (recall-biased) → ≤10 findings`

You are reviewing for **recall** at high effort: catch every real bug a careful
reviewer would catch in one sitting. At this level, catching real bugs matters
more than avoiding false positives. Err on the side of surfacing.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle + 1 conventions angle, up to 6 each)

Run **8 independent finder angles** via the Agent tool. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

### Conventions (CLAUDE.md)

Find the CLAUDE.md files that govern the changed code: the user-level
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the repo-root CLAUDE.md, plus any CLAUDE.md or
CLAUDE.local.md in a directory that is an ancestor of a changed file (a
directory's CLAUDE.md only applies to files at or below it). Read each one
that exists, then check the diff for clear violations of the rules they state.

Only flag a violation when you can quote the exact rule and the exact line
that breaks it — no style preferences, no vague "spirit of the doc"
inferences. In the finding, name the CLAUDE.md path and quote the rule so the
report can cite it. If no CLAUDE.md applies, return nothing for this angle.

Cleanup, altitude, and conventions candidates use the same
`file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in `failure_scenario`, state the concrete
cost (what is duplicated, wasted, harder to maintain, or which CLAUDE.md rule
is broken) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank cleanup,
altitude, and conventions findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates bypass the verify step and are the
dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Verify (1-vote, recall-biased)

Dedup near-duplicates (same defect, same location, same reason → keep one). For
each remaining candidate, run **one verifier** via the Agent tool:
give it the diff, the relevant file(s), and the candidate; it returns exactly
one of **CONFIRMED / PLAUSIBLE / REFUTED**.

**PLAUSIBLE by default** — do not refute a candidate for being "speculative" or
"depends on runtime state" when the state is realistic: concurrency races,
nil/undefined on a rare-but-reachable path (error handler, cold cache, missing
optional field), falsy-zero treated as missing, off-by-one on a boundary the
code does not exclude, retry storms / partial failures, regex/allowlist that
lost an anchor. These are PLAUSIBLE.

**REFUTED** only when constructible from the code: factually wrong (quote the
actual line); provably impossible (type/constant/invariant — show it); already
handled in this diff (cite the guard); or pure style with no observable effect.

Keep **CONFIRMED and PLAUSIBLE**. Drop REFUTED.

## Output

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 10 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 10 survive, keep the 10 most
severe. If nothing survives verification, return `[]`.

  },
  {
    "file": "/Users/raman/projects/lock_code_manager/custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/_base.py",

Extracting a tiny `_read_pin_capability()` returning `(caps, pin)` removes the copy-pasted `caps = ...; pin = caps[...].get(_PIN_TYPE_STR)` pair and makes the recovery re-read a single call.",

  },
  {
    "file": "/Users/raman/projects/lock_code_manager/tests/providers/zwave_js/test_provider.py",
    "line": 740,
    "summary": "`_zero_slot_caps()` and `_healthy_caps()` are near-identical dict builders that differ only in the PIN block's `num_slots` (0 vs 30) — every other field (supports_user_management, max_users, supported_user_types, credential rules, min/max length, supports_learn) is duplicated verbatim.     "failure_scenario": "Two ~15-line literal dicts that must stay structurally identical for the tests to be comparing like-with-like.   },
  {

    "line": 968,

    "failure_scenario": "The degraded-capability fixture and the 'force LOADED' dance are copied per-test; a change to how a lock detects its integration is loaded (e.g. checking more than `state`) forces edits in every copy, and the inconsistency risk means one test can quietly diverge and pass for the wrong reason."

<summary>Agent "Angle: efficiency finder" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>Confirmed: no persistent state gates the recovery. The docstring claims "one-shot," but it is only one-shot *within a single `async_get_capabilities` call* — nothing prevents it re-running on every probe. Here are my findings.

```json
[
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/zwave_js.py",
    "line": 296,
    "summary": "Zero-slot recovery (getUsersCount device query + a second full capability re-read) has no once-per-lifetime gate or backoff; it re-runs on every async_get_capabilities miss.",
    "failure_scenario": "A genuinely zero-slot lock (interview permanently incomplete, or driver < 15.24.3) that answers getUsersCount but still reports 0 slots will never recover. Yet because the cache is invalidated on failure and _async_on_integration_loaded re-runs _async_run_provider_setup -> _get_cached_capabilities on EVERY zwave_js config-entry LOADED transition, each reconnect/reload of a flapping zwave_js integration re-probes the device: 1 getUsersCount radio round-trip + 2 full async_get_credential_capabilities reads, forever, hammering a battery lock's radio. Cheaper: set an instance flag (e.g. _attempted_slot_recovery) so the getUsersCount device query runs at most once per provider instance lifetime, matching the 'one-shot' the docstring already claims."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/_base.py",
    "line": 769,
    "summary": "Invalidating _capabilities_cache on validation failure, combined with revalidation on every provider LOADED transition, forces a fresh full device capability probe for every degraded lock on each reconnect/reload.",
    "failure_scenario": "Setting self._capabilities_cache = None on the no-PIN branch means a lock that is structurally degraded never caches a result. _async_on_integration_loaded re-runs _async_run_provider_setup unconditionally on each LOADED transition, so every degraded lock re-issues its full async_get_capabilities device read (for zwave_js, that is the 3-round-trip recovery sequence above) on every zwave_js reconnect and every LCM reload. At startup with N degraded locks this is N repeated blocking device reads with no backoff. Cheaper: cache the negative/last-known capabilities and only re-probe on an explicit, rate-limited signal rather than clearing the cache so every revalidation path re-hits the radio."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/zwave_js.py",
    "line": 289,
    "summary": "The recovery success path performs two sequential full credential-capability reads plus the getUsersCount query — three sequential device round-trips — on the same probe.",
    "failure_scenario": "async_get_capabilities calls _async_read_credential_capabilities (read 1), then _async_recover_user_code_slot_count (getUsersCount device query), then _async_read_credential_capabilities again (read 2), all awaited sequentially. Every zero-slot probe pays 3 serialized radio round-trips on a slow/battery Z-Wave node. On a lock that stays degenerate after the query this is pure waste (read 2 returns the same 0). Cheaper: skip the unconditional second full capability read — read back only the User Code CC supportedUsers value the query repopulated, or short-circuit the re-read when getUsersCount reported the same count."
  },
  {
    "file": "tests/providers/test_base.py",
    "line": 452,
    "summary": "The new repair-issue tests each drive a full async_setup_internal (coordinator creation + async_refresh + push wiring + async_shutdown/async_unload teardown) to assert on nothing but the issue registry.",
    "failure_scenario": "test_setup_validation_failure_raises_and_clears_repair_issue, test_reconnect_validation_failure_raises_repair_issue, and test_unload_permanently_clears_setup_failed_repair_issue only verify that _report_setup_validation_failure / _clear_setup_validation_issue create and delete the repair issue, but each spins up and tears down a real LockUsercodeUpdateCoordinator (an async refresh cycle) per test. Cheaper: exercise _report_setup_validation_failure / _clear_setup_validation_issue (or _async_run_provider_setup) directly against the issue registry without standing up and shutting down the coordinator, reserving the full setup path for the one degrade-and-recover test that actually asserts coordinator existence."
  }
]
```</result>
<usage><subagent_tokens>74911</subagent_tokens><tool_uses>10</tool_uses><duration_ms>120742</duration_ms></usage>
</task-notification>

<result>Based on my analysis of the diff and surrounding code, here are the findings:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/__init__.py",
    "line": 541,
    "summary": "Options-preferred merge in _setup_entry_after_start assumes options is always newer than data, but the reauth flow writes fresh config to data (config_flow.py:511-512, `data={**config_entry.data, **user_input}`) — the merge papers over the real defect (no update listener registered while the entry is failed) rather than fixing the lifecycle.",
    "failure_scenario": "User saves an options-flow fix while the entry is down (lands in options), then later fixes the SAME entry via the reauth flow (fresh config written to data) and reload. On the successful reload the merge `{**data, **options}` prefers the stale earlier options over the newer reauth data, silently reverting the reauth fix. The 'options is newest' invariant that EntryConfig.from_entry relies on is not enforced anywhere in the state machine; the deeper fix is to register the update listener / clear options even for a failed entry, or have reauth write through the same precedence."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/_base.py",
    "line": 884,
    "summary": "lock_setup_failed_{lock_entity_id} is a globally-keyed per-lock issue but is raised and cleared from per-BaseLock-instance methods. async_unload(remove_permanently) clears it unconditionally, without the `_lock_managed_by_other_entry` guard that async_remove_entry (__init__.py:796) uses for the very same id.",
    "failure_scenario": "Two LCM entries manage the same physical lock and both raised lock_setup_failed. The user removes the lock from entry A only; A's async_unload(True) deletes the shared global issue, dismissing entry B's still-valid repair. Because lock_setup_failed is only re-raised on setup/LOADED (unlike lock_offline, which any coordinator poll re-raises), B's repair stays gone until B is reloaded/reconnected. Repair lifecycle is split across _base.py (raise/clear), coordinator.py (lock_offline), and __init__.py (bulk cleanup), so the id/guard logic drifts between the removal path and the unload/recovery path."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/zwave_js.py",
    "line": 376,
    "summary": "The getUsersCount recovery awaits a synchronous device query (async_invoke_cc_api) on a node that is, by the code's own comment, typically a battery lock whose interview completed while ASLEEP — exactly the node most likely to not respond promptly.",
    "failure_scenario": "For a sleeping battery lock the queued getUsersCount command resolves only when the node next wakes (potentially hours) or after a server-side timeout. Because this runs inside async_get_capabilities → _get_cached_capabilities → _async_run_provider_setup, it can stall the LCM setup/validation coroutine on the sleeping lock instead of degrading fast and revalidating on the next reconnect/LOADED transition. The one-shot poke is a provider-specific bandaid layered on the base validation path; the retry/recovery belongs in the base's reconnect machinery (or upstream in HA lock_helpers, where a solicited UsersNumberReport could repopulate supportedUsers) rather than a blocking device query at setup time."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/zwave_js.py",
    "line": 376,
    "summary": "Hardcoded `self.node.endpoints[0]` for the User Code CC invoke assumes the CC lives on the root endpoint; _node_advertises_user_code_cc similarly only inspects `self.node.command_classes` (endpoint 0), while lock_helpers.async_get_credential_capabilities derives the correct endpoint internally.",
    "failure_scenario": "On a multi-endpoint Z-Wave lock whose User Code CC is exposed on a non-root endpoint, both the capability check and the getUsersCount poke target endpoint 0 and miss the CC, so recovery either no-ops or errors on an endpoint that doesn't advertise the CC — and the provider raises the 'no usable PIN slots' structural error for a lock that is actually fine. The endpoint should be derived the same way the rest of the User Code CC surface derives it, not hardcoded to 0."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/__init__.py",
    "line": 798,
    "summary": "async_remove_entry must statically enumerate every per-lock issue id (lock_offline, lock_setup_failed) that is raised by other layers; there is no single per-lock repair registry/helper, so each newly-added issue type must be manually mirrored here or it leaks.",
    "failure_scenario": "lock_setup_failed is raised in _base.py, lock_offline in coordinator.py, slot repairs elsewhere, and cleanup is duplicated across async_remove_entry, the async_unload path, and the recovery clears. The next per-lock repair added anywhere will orphan on entry removal unless a developer remembers to add another `async_delete_issue(...f\"..._{lock_entity_id}\")` line here — the id strings are the only coupling. A per-lock issue helper that owns id construction, the _lock_managed_by_other_entry guard, and cleanup would remove this scattering."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/_base.py",
    "line": 769,
    "summary": "The degenerate-cache invalidation (`self._capabilities_cache = None`) lives only in the base PIN-validation branch, so it only fires for providers that RETURN a degenerate-but-successful LockCapabilities (matter/seam). The zwave provider raises inside async_get_capabilities instead, and the invalidation contract between the two paths is implicit.",
    "failure_scenario": "The recovery correctness depends on a subtle split: matter/seam rely on line 769 to drop a cached no-PIN read, while zwave relies on async_get_capabilities raising (so _get_cached_capabilities never caches). If a future provider returns degenerate caps from a code path other than the base PIN check (e.g. a slot-only branch that later proves unusable), the stale successful capability read is served to every revalidation and the lock can never recover without a full reload — the exact failure this diff set out to fix. The cache-invalidation-on-degenerate-read belongs inside _get_cached_capabilities / the retry machinery, not duplicated per validation branch."
  }
]
```</result>

    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/__init__.py",
    "line": 541,
    "summary": "Options-preferred merge in _setup_entry_after_start assumes options is always newer than data, but the reauth flow writes fresh config to data (config_flow.py:511-512, `data={**config_entry.data, **user_input}`) — the merge papers over the real defect (no update listener registered while the entry is failed) rather than fixing the lifecycle.",
    "failure_scenario": "User saves an options-flow fix while the entry is down (lands in options), then later fixes the SAME entry via the reauth flow (fresh config written to data) and reload. On the successful reload the merge `{**data, **options}` prefers the stale earlier options over the newer reauth data, silently reverting the reauth fix. The 'options is newest' invariant that EntryConfig.from_entry relies on is not enforced anywhere in the state machine; the deeper fix is to register the update listener / clear options even for a failed entry, or have reauth write through the same precedence."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/_base.py",
    "line": 884,
    "summary": "lock_setup_failed_{lock_entity_id} is a globally-keyed per-lock issue but is raised and cleared from per-BaseLock-instance methods. async_unload(remove_permanently) clears it unconditionally, without the `_lock_managed_by_other_entry` guard that async_remove_entry (__init__.py:796) uses for the very same id.",
    "failure_scenario": "Two LCM entries manage the same physical lock and both raised lock_setup_failed. The user removes the lock from entry A only; A's async_unload(True) deletes the shared global issue, dismissing entry B's still-valid repair. Because lock_setup_failed is only re-raised on setup/LOADED (unlike lock_offline, which any coordinator poll re-raises), B's repair stays gone until B is reloaded/reconnected. Repair lifecycle is split across _base.py (raise/clear), coordinator.py (lock_offline), and __init__.py (bulk cleanup), so the id/guard logic drifts between the removal path and the unload/recovery path."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/zwave_js.py",
    "line": 376,
    "summary": "The getUsersCount recovery awaits a synchronous device query (async_invoke_cc_api) on a node that is, by the code's own comment, typically a battery lock whose interview completed while ASLEEP — exactly the node most likely to not respond promptly.",
    "failure_scenario": "For a sleeping battery lock the queued getUsersCount command resolves only when the node next wakes (potentially hours) or after a server-side timeout. Because this runs inside async_get_capabilities → _get_cached_capabilities → _async_run_provider_setup, it can stall the LCM setup/validation coroutine on the sleeping lock instead of degrading fast and revalidating on the next reconnect/LOADED transition. The one-shot poke is a provider-specific bandaid layered on the base validation path; the retry/recovery belongs in the base's reconnect machinery (or upstream in HA lock_helpers, where a solicited UsersNumberReport could repopulate supportedUsers) rather than a blocking device query at setup time."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/zwave_js.py",
    "line": 376,
    "summary": "Hardcoded `self.node.endpoints[0]` for the User Code CC invoke assumes the CC lives on the root endpoint; _node_advertises_user_code_cc similarly only inspects `self.node.command_classes` (endpoint 0), while lock_helpers.async_get_credential_capabilities derives the correct endpoint internally.",
    "failure_scenario": "On a multi-endpoint Z-Wave lock whose User Code CC is exposed on a non-root endpoint, both the capability check and the getUsersCount poke target endpoint 0 and miss the CC, so recovery either no-ops or errors on an endpoint that doesn't advertise the CC — and the provider raises the 'no usable PIN slots' structural error for a lock that is actually fine. The endpoint should be derived the same way the rest of the User Code CC surface derives it, not hardcoded to 0."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/__init__.py",
    "line": 798,
    "summary": "async_remove_entry must statically enumerate every per-lock issue id (lock_offline, lock_setup_failed) that is raised by other layers; there is no single per-lock repair registry/helper, so each newly-added issue type must be manually mirrored here or it leaks.",
    "failure_scenario": "lock_setup_failed is raised in _base.py, lock_offline in coordinator.py, slot repairs elsewhere, and cleanup is duplicated across async_remove_entry, the async_unload path, and the recovery clears. The next per-lock repair added anywhere will orphan on entry removal unless a developer remembers to add another `async_delete_issue(...f\"..._{lock_entity_id}\")` line here — the id strings are the only coupling. A per-lock issue helper that owns id construction, the _lock_managed_by_other_entry guard, and cleanup would remove this scattering."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/_base.py",
    "line": 769,
    "summary": "The degenerate-cache invalidation (`self._capabilities_cache = None`) lives only in the base PIN-validation branch, so it only fires for providers that RETURN a degenerate-but-successful LockCapabilities (matter/seam). The zwave provider raises inside async_get_capabilities instead, and the invalidation contract between the two paths is implicit.",
    "failure_scenario": "The recovery correctness depends on a subtle split: matter/seam rely on line 769 to drop a cached no-PIN read, while zwave relies on async_get_capabilities raising (so _get_cached_capabilities never caches). If a future provider returns degenerate caps from a code path other than the base PIN check (e.g. a slot-only branch that later proves unusable), the stale successful capability read is served to every revalidation and the lock can never recover without a full reload — the exact failure this diff set out to fix. The cache-invalidation-on-degenerate-read belongs inside _get_cached_capabilities / the retry machinery, not duplicated per validation branch."
  }

<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>Based on my line-by-line scan of the diff and the enclosing functions in the actual repo files, here are the candidate findings.

```json
[
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/zwave_js.py",
    "line": 379,
    "summary": "Recovery query catches only BaseZwaveJSServerError, not HomeAssistantError — escape drops the lock",
    "failure_scenario": "A User Code CC battery lock reports 0 PIN slots, so _async_recover_user_code_slot_count runs node.endpoints[0].async_invoke_cc_api('getUsersCount'). Every other provider I/O in this file catches BOTH BaseZwaveJSServerError AND HomeAssistantError (e.g. lines 346/348, 424/426, 966/968), but this new method catches only BaseZwaveJSServerError. If the HA zwave_js layer raises a HomeAssistantError (node/driver not ready), it propagates uncaught out of async_get_capabilities → _get_cached_capabilities → _async_run_provider_setup. async_setup_internal only catches LockDisconnected/LockOperationFailed/LockCodeManagerProviderError, so the raw HomeAssistantError escapes to _async_setup_new_locks, which pops (drops) the lock — the exact invisible-lock/no-retry outcome this PR set out to prevent."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/zwave_js.py",
    "line": 376,
    "summary": "node.endpoints[0] lookup in recovery can raise KeyError/IndexError that escapes and drops the lock",
    "failure_scenario": "If node.endpoints has no key 0 (node model not fully populated at the moment the zero-slot probe fires during a slow interview), self.node.endpoints[0] raises KeyError. That is not a BaseZwaveJSServerError, so it is not caught in _async_recover_user_code_slot_count and propagates out of async_get_capabilities → async_setup_internal (uncaught) → _async_setup_new_locks pops the lock instead of degrading it."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/_base.py",
    "line": 844,
    "summary": "_async_on_integration_loaded structural-failure branch never resets _setup_succeeded to False",
    "failure_scenario": "A zwave lock initially sets up successfully (_setup_succeeded=True). Later a re-interview corrupts/zeroes the slot count; the provider integration LOADED transition re-runs _async_run_provider_setup, which raises LockCodeManagerProviderError. The except branch reports the repair issue but leaves _setup_succeeded=True (stale). The lock is now degraded and has a repair issue open, yet internal state still claims setup succeeded; a subsequent genuine recovery won't emit the 'Provider setup succeeded' transition log (guarded by `if not self._setup_succeeded` at line 846). Impact is limited to logging today, but the state flag is now inconsistent with reality."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/zwave_js.py",
    "line": 296,
    "summary": "getUsersCount device query re-issued on every reconnect for a permanently-degraded lock",
    "failure_scenario": "For a lock that is genuinely stuck at zero usable PIN slots (old Z-Wave JS driver < 15.24.3, or an interview that never completes), async_get_capabilities runs on initial setup AND on every provider integration LOADED/reconnect transition (_async_on_integration_loaded → _async_run_provider_setup → _get_cached_capabilities re-probes since the cache was invalidated). Each pass re-invokes the getUsersCount device query, waking a battery lock and logging an INFO line every time. Over repeated zwave_js reconnects/reloads this becomes repeated device wakeups (battery drain) and log spam despite the 'one-shot' framing in the docstring — the query is one-shot per capability read, not per lock lifetime."
  }
]
```</result>
<usage><subagent_tokens>91752</subagent_tokens><tool_uses>24</tool_uses><duration_ms>343975</duration_ms></usage>
</task-notification>

Construct the concrete event sequence and determine whether the stale-options-wins outcome is reachable. Also consider: after a successful normal update-listener pass, options is reset to {} (async_update_entry(..., data=new_config.to_dict(), options={})) — so for the candidate to fire, the options-flow save AND the reauth must both happen while the entry never successfully loaded in between. Is that realistic in the exclusion→reauth scenario this PR targets?

<summary>Agent "Verify: unload guard mismatch" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>REFUTED.

Every path to `BaseLock.async_unload(True)` funnels through `async_unload_lock` at `__init__.py:666-671`, where `await lock.async_unload(remove_permanently)` executes only inside `if not _lock_managed_by_other_entry(hass, config_entry, _lock_entity_id)`. The sole caller that passes `remove_permanently=True` is the update-listener `locks_to_remove` path (`__init__.py:1041-1042`); the only other caller, `async_unload_entry` (`:758`), passes the default `False`. So when entry A drops a lock that loaded entry B still manages, `_lock_managed_by_other_entry` (which scans other non-disabled/non-ignored entries' configs via `has_lock`, `:613-625`) returns True, and `lock.async_unload(...)` — and therefore `_clear_setup_validation_issue()` on the shared BaseLock instance (`_find_shared_lock_instance`, `:628-652`) — is never invoked, leaving the `lock_setup_failed_{lock_entity_id}` repair intact for B. The candidate's premise (async_unload(True) running while another entry manages the lock) is exactly what that gate blocks, so the bug is not reachable.</result>

<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>CONFIRMED. In `_async_on_integration_loaded` (`_base.py:838-844`), the `except LockCodeManagerProviderError` branch reports the repair issue but never sets `self._setup_succeeded = False`, so a previously-successful lock that later fails structural validation on a LOADED transition retains the stale `_setup_succeeded = True` while a `lock_setup_failed` repair is open — a genuine internal-state inconsistency.

However, the only behavioral consequence is a missing "Provider setup succeeded" info log on eventual recovery: the sole reads of `_setup_succeeded` are the two log/issue guards at lines 702 and 846, and it gates nothing functional (coordinator refresh, push subscription, and entity availability all proceed unconditionally). Notably the repair issue is still cleared on recovery, since `_clear_setup_validation_issue()` at line 852 runs unconditionally, not under the `if not self._setup_succeeded` guard. The initial-setup path (lines 692-700) has the same non-reset asymmetry, but there it is harmless because `_setup_succeeded` defaults to False. So: real-but-minor — the only impact is a missing log line.</result>

<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Verify: recovery except coverage" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>I have enough to render a verdict. Key findings:

- `NotFoundError` (exceptions.py line 49) IS `class NotFoundError(BaseZwaveJSServerError)` — a subclass. `async_invoke_cc_api` (endpoint.py line 217) raises exactly this when the CC is absent, so it is CAUGHT by the recovery method's `except BaseZwaveJSServerError`. Claim (d) is wrong.
- The library send path (`client.async_send_command`, client.py ~113) just does `return await future` with no `asyncio.wait_for`/timeout wrapper; results are resolved via `FailedCommand`/other `BaseZwaveJSServerError` subclasses. No `asyncio.TimeoutError` is constructible from this call. Claim (c) is wrong.
- Nothing in this call chain (`async_invoke_cc_api` → `async_send_command` → client) raises `HomeAssistantError`; the only HA-flavored error is `self.node` raising `LockDisconnected` (zwave_js.py line 157), which IS in `async_setup_internal`'s caught set (`except (LockDisconnected, LockOperationFailed)`, _base.py line 683). Claim (b) does not reach the silent-drop path.
- `node.endpoints` is a plain `dict[int, Endpoint]` (node/__init__.py line 127), so `self.node.endpoints[0]` (zwave_js.py line 376) is an unguarded subscription. A `KeyError` here is neither `BaseZwaveJSServerError` nor one of the three exceptions `async_setup_internal` catches, so it WOULD escape to `_async_setup_new_locks` and pop the lock. But endpoint 0 (the root endpoint) is treated as universally present across the library (`node.command_classes`, `node.async_invoke_cc_api`, etc. all do `self.endpoints[0]` unguarded), so its absence is not a realistic state.

VERDICT: PLAUSIBLE — Three of the four named mechanisms are provably not constructible: NotFoundError subclasses BaseZwaveJSServerError (so it is caught), the library's websocket send has no timeout wrapper (no asyncio.TimeoutError), and no layer in this chain raises HomeAssistantError except self.node's LockDisconnected which async_setup_internal already catches. The only genuine unhandled escape is a KeyError from the unguarded `self.node.endpoints[0]` dict subscription, which would indeed bypass all three caught types and produce the silent drop — but endpoint 0 is assumed always-present throughout zwave-js-server-python, making this a real-but-improbable state rather than a demonstrable bug.</result>
<usage><subagent_tokens>57572</subagent_tokens><tool_uses>15</tool_uses><duration_ms>99685</duration_ms></usage>
</task-notification>

Claim (a) CONFIRMED exactly: the `f"lock_setup_failed_{entity_id}"` id string is hand-built at 3 sites with no shared builder/constant — `providers/_base.py:788` (create), `providers/_base.py:804` (clear), and `__init__.py:798` (removal cleanup). The sibling `lock_offline` id is likewise built at 3 sites (`domain/coordinator.py:331` create, `:367` delete, `__init__.py:797` removal).

Claim (c) CONFIRMED: `__init__.py:791-804` statically re-enumerates every persistent issue id (`slot_disabled`, `pin_required`, `lock_offline`, `lock_setup_failed`, `slot_suspended`) by rebuilding the f-strings inline, so a new per-lock repair type orphans a persistent issue if the developer forgets to add its delete line here — a concrete, real maintenance cost.

Claim (b) is OVERSTATED, which is why this is PLAUSIBLE not CONFIRMED: only 2 sites are true per-lock, `is_fixable=False`, `lock_entity_id`-keyed issues (`lock_setup_failed` in `_base.py`, `lock_offline` in `coordinator.py:328`). The other cited blocks — `util.py:147` slot_disabled, `sync.py:484` slot_disabled, `sync.py:517` slot_suspended, `slot_coordinator.py:409` pin_required — are all `is_fixable=True`, keyed per-slot (`entry_id_slot_num`), with different placeholders; they share only the generic HA `async_create_issue` call shape, not the specific per-lock pattern. So the real consolidation win is a modest id-builder plus a per-lock create/delete helper (guarded by `_lock_managed_by_other_entry`) covering 2 issue types — a legitimate but small cleanup, not the 5-6-copy dedup the finding implies.</result>

The core mechanism is real: there is no per-lock-lifetime gate. `_async_recover_user_code_slot_count` (zwave_js.py:351) fires unconditionally whenever `async_get_capabilities` (zwave_js.py:296) hits the zero-slot branch, and on a still-degenerate lock `async_get_capabilities` raises `LockCodeManagerProviderError` (line 324) *before* `_get_cached_capabilities` (_base.py:1300-1309) can assign `_capabilities_cache`, so the cache stays `None` and every future probe re-enters `async_get_capabilities` → one `getUsersCount` invoke plus the two `_async_read_credential_capabilities` reads (lines 289/298) each pass. Path (b) is confirmed: the write seam `_set_credential`/`_delete_credential` (_base.py:1474/1526) calls `_assert_credential_type_supported` → `_get_cached_capabilities` first, so each write attempt re-fires the query then aborts.

Two sub-claims are REFUTED. (c) is wrong: the coordinator read path (`async_get_usercodes` → `_project_users_to_slots` → `async_get_users`, _base.py:1278-1286) never consults `_get_cached_capabilities`, so refreshes do not re-probe. The "genuinely SLEEPING node stalls indefinitely" sub-claim is also refuted by `async_send_command` (endpoint.py:187-206): with `wait_for_result=None` (the recovery passes no override), an already ASLEEP/DEAD node routes to `async_send_command_no_wait` and returns immediately, and an awake node that falls asleep mid-command has its result task cancelled via `status_event` — the design specifically avoids the indefinite await for non-FLiRS sleepers.</result>

<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Verify: reauth vs options merge" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>CONFIRMED

Verified sequence and code. The failed-entry window is real and never clears options: `async_setup_entry` (`__init__.py:557-568`) reads `get_entry_config` (which is `EntryConfig.from_entry`, options-preferred, `domain/config.py:57-66`); when a referenced lock is missing it calls `async_start_reauth` and raises `ConfigEntryError` *before* reaching `_setup_entry_after_start` (line 589), so while the entry is failed no update listener is registered and no data→options migration/normalization runs — an options-flow save just sits in `options` with no reload (`async_update_listener` never fires). `async_step_reauth` (`config_flow.py:511-517`) writes the new lock list into DATA (`data={**config_entry.data, **user_input}`), leaves `options` untouched, then reloads. On that reload, both the missing-lock check and the new merge `options={**config_entry.data, **config_entry.options}` (`__init__.py:538-542`) are options-preferred, so the stale options-flow config wins and the reauth's data-side lock fix is silently discarded — exactly the claimed reversion. This is reachable because the entry stays in SETUP_ERROR the whole time (no successful load resets options to `{}` between the two edits, per the listener's `data=new_config.to_dict(), options={}` at line 1090-1091); the only narrowing is that the stale options must carry a valid-but-different lock set (otherwise the missing-lock check re-fails before the merge), which is a plausible "user tried fixing it two ways" case. No step provably clears options first, so this is not refuted.</result>

<summary>Agent "Verify: rename orphans repair" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>REFUTED. `self.lock` is a frozen `er.RegistryEntry` fetched in `async_create_lock_instance` (domain/locks.py:34, `ent_reg.async_get(lock_entity_id)`) using the entity-id string taken from `config.locks`, so `self.lock.entity_id` is always that config string, and there is no `EVENT_ENTITY_REGISTRY_UPDATED` listener rewriting `config.locks` on rename — meaning both the raise path (`_report_setup_validation_failure`, keyed off `self.lock.entity_id`) and every clear path (`_clear_setup_validation_issue`/`async_unload(True)` via the same stale snapshot, and `async_remove_entry` iterating `config.locks`) resolve to the SAME old id; the candidate's premise that clears "compute the NEW id" is false. In fact a rename makes `async_setup_entry`'s missing-entity check (`__init__.py:557-568`, `ent_reg.async_get(old_id)` now returns None) fire reauth + `ConfigEntryError` so LCM can't even load under the new id until the user reconfigures, further preventing the divergence. Separately, the pre-existing `lock_offline` issue uses an identical keying/clear pattern (coordinator.py:331 raise via `self._lock.lock.entity_id`, cleared in the same `async_remove_entry` `config.locks` loop at `__init__.py:797`), so even if a residual edge case existed it would be an extension of a pre-existing pattern, not a new class of bug — and non-fixable persistent HA issues still expose an "ignore" affordance, so "survives forever with no dismissal" is overstated.</result>

content.text:
PR #1345 is now MERGEABLE with a clean 6-file diff (main merged in, conflicts resolved keeping the stacked changes, full suite green, pushed).

## Code review results

8 findings survived verification (11 candidates verified, 4 refuted — including the shared-lock unload guard, the entity-rename orphan, and the missing-integration-test claims, each disproven by citing the actual guards/tests).

```json
[
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/domain/sync.py",
    "line": 836,
    "summary": "Degraded locks still run sync ticks: a structural capability failure lands in the generic 'except Exception' and raises a misleading 'may indicate a bug — report this issue' slot_suspended repair (zwave), or disables the slot outright (matter), while the new lock_setup_failed repair text falsely claims 'code synchronization is paused for this lock'",
    "failure_scenario": "Re-included BE469ZP kept degraded per #1344; binary_sensor.py starts its sync manager unconditionally and nothing gates ticks on _setup_succeeded. First OUT_OF_SYNC tick → _set_credential → _assert_credential_type_supported → async_get_capabilities raises bare LockCodeManagerProviderError → sync.py:836 generic handler suspends the slot with 'report this bug' text stacked on the accurate lock_setup_failed repair. On matter, empty credential_types instead raises CodeRejectedError → _disable_slot flips the active switch off, so recovery also needs manual per-slot re-enable. CONFIRMED (all four sub-claims)."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/zwave_js.py",
    "line": 296,
    "summary": "The 'one-shot' getUsersCount recovery has no per-lock-lifetime gate: a lock that stays at zero slots re-fires the device query (plus two full capability reads) on every zwave_js reload/reconnect AND every sync write attempt",
    "failure_scenario": "async_get_capabilities raises while still degenerate, so _capabilities_cache is never populated; every _get_cached_capabilities miss re-enters the recovery. Combined with finding 1, each sync tick's write attempt radio-queries a battery lock and logs INFO, indefinitely, on a lock that will never recover (e.g. driver < 15.24.3). CONFIRMED — cadence traced: per LOADED transition and per _set_credential/_delete_credential; coordinator reads do NOT re-probe, and the sleeping-node-stall concern is refuted (async_send_command routes asleep nodes to no-wait)."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/__init__.py",
    "line": 541,
    "summary": "The options-preferred migration merge can silently revert a reauth fix: reauth writes its corrected lock list into data, but stale options saved earlier during the same failed-entry window win the merge",
    "failure_scenario": "Entry in SETUP_ERROR after an exclusion → user saves an options-flow change (sits unprocessed in options, never cleared) → user then completes the reauth flow, which writes the new lock list to data and reloads → _setup_entry_after_start merges {**data, **options} and the stale options overwrite the reauth fix. CONFIRMED — no step clears options between the two edits; only narrowing is the stale options must reference locks that still exist."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/__init__.py",
    "line": 798,
    "summary": "Per-lock repair ids are hand-built f-strings at 3 sites each (create/clear/remove-entry) with no shared builder, and async_remove_entry must statically enumerate every per-lock issue type — the next repair type added anywhere will orphan a persistent issue unless its delete line is remembered here",
    "failure_scenario": "lock_setup_failed_{id} built independently at _base.py:788, _base.py:804, __init__.py:798 (lock_offline has the same 3-site split). A modest per-lock issue helper owning id construction, the _lock_managed_by_other_entry guard, and cleanup removes the drift risk. PLAUSIBLE — scoped to the 2 per-lock issue types; the slot-level repairs cited by the finder use a different shape."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/_base.py",
    "line": 700,
    "summary": "The 'else: if self._setup_succeeded: _clear_setup_validation_issue()' clause re-derives success from the flag; clearing inline right after '_setup_succeeded = True' is strictly equivalent and deletes the clause",
    "failure_scenario": "The success side-effect lives away from where success is established; a future edit adding another success exit or reordering the deferred branch must keep the guard in sync or it will spuriously clear/keep the repair. CONFIRMED equivalent on all three paths (single-call-per-instance invariant verified)."
  },
  {
    "file": "tests/providers/zwave_js/test_provider.py",
    "line": 723,
    "summary": "_zero_slot_caps()/_healthy_caps() are ~18-line literals identical except num_slots, and _healthy_caps diverges from the canonical mock_lock_helpers fixture caps in four fields — the recovery test validates a caps shape no fixture produces",
    "failure_scenario": "A schema change updating the conftest fixture leaves these stale while the tests stay green against a dead shape. A single _caps(num_slots=...) factory (ideally shared with/derived from the conftest default) collapses all three sites. CONFIRMED."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/_base.py",
    "line": 838,
    "summary": "_async_on_integration_loaded's validation-failure branch never resets _setup_succeeded to False, leaving stale internal state when a previously-healthy lock later fails revalidation",
    "failure_scenario": "Lock sets up fine, later re-interview zeroes its slot count, LOADED transition fails validation and opens the repair — but _setup_succeeded stays True, so the eventual genuine recovery skips the 'Provider setup succeeded' log. CONFIRMED, minor: the flag gates nothing functional today (repair clearing runs unconditionally), but the state is inconsistent with reality."
  },
  {
    "file": "custom_components/lock_code_manager/providers/zwave_js.py",
    "line": 376,
    "summary": "self.node.endpoints[0] is an unguarded dict subscription inside the recovery; a KeyError is not BaseZwaveJSServerError and would escape all of async_setup_internal's handlers, dropping the lock",
    "failure_scenario": "If endpoint 0 were ever absent from the node model, the KeyError propagates to _async_setup_new_locks which pops the lock — the exact silent drop the PR eliminates. PLAUSIBLE but improbable: zwave-js-server-python itself subscripts endpoints[0] unguarded everywhere, and the other claimed escape routes (HomeAssistantError, TimeoutError, NotFoundError) were each proven impossible."
  }
]
```

**Where to fix:** findings 1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 8 live in #1344's merged code → new branch. Findings 4 and 5 (and the strings-text half of finding 1) are #1345's code → can be fixed on its open branch.

**Suggested grouping:** findings 1+2 are really one design gap — a degraded lock needs a *sync gate* (e.g. skip ticks / mark slots unavailable while `_setup_succeeded` is False) which simultaneously stops the misleading repairs, makes the "synchronization is paused" text true, and bounds the recovery re-fires to reconnect transitions only. Finding 3 wants the reauth flow to write through the same options-preferred channel (or clear options when it writes data).

Want me to fix these — findings 1/2/3/6/7/8 on a new branch and 4/5 on the #1345 branch?

input.content:
---
name: project-degraded-lock-followups
description: Post-#1344/#1345 review findings — sync gating for structurally-degraded locks, reauth/options precedence, recovery re-fire cadence
metadata:
  type: project
---

Context (2026-07-15): forum post 86 (Craig/AA6CG, factory-reset BE469ZP) led to PR #1344 (merged: zero-slot getUsersCount recovery, degrade-not-drop, options-preferred migration merge) and PR #1345 (open: lock_setup_failed repair issue, stacked then retargeted to main). A high-effort multi-agent review of both produced 8 verified findings; raman approved fixing all of them.

Fix placement:
- New branch (code merged via #1344): (1) gate sync ticks on structural setup failure — degraded locks currently still tick, producing misleading "report this bug" slot_suspended repairs (zwave) / slot_disabled+switch-off (matter), and making the #1345 repair text "synchronization is paused" false; gating also bounds the getUsersCount recovery re-fires to reconnect transitions (2) reauth flow writes CONF_LOCKS into data while stale options win the new migration merge — reauth should write options-preferred and clear options (3) clear repair inline after `_setup_succeeded = True` instead of the else-clause re-derivation (4) reset `_setup_succeeded = False` in _async_on_integration_loaded's validation-failure branch (5) `endpoints.get(0)` guard in zwave recovery (6) collapse _zero_slot_caps/_healthy_caps test builders into one _caps(num_slots) factory.
- #1345 branch: shared per-lock repair-issue id builder + async_remove_entry iterating a per-lock issue-key tuple instead of hand-enumerated f-strings (lock_offline + lock_setup_failed each build ids at 3 independent sites).

Verified-refuted (do not re-flag): async_unload(True) shared-lock guard (async_unload_lock gates it), entity-rename repair orphan (snapshot keying is self-consistent), sleeping-node stall in recovery (async_send_command routes asleep nodes to no-wait), NotFoundError/HomeAssistantError/TimeoutError escapes from invoke_cc_api (only KeyError constructible).

Key driver fact (also in repo comments): UserCodeCC.refreshValues reads supportedUsers from cache (`?? 0`) and never re-queries it; only the CC API getUsersCount device query repopulates it. Related: [[project-zwave-uc-zero-slots-root-cause]].

139	                    new_slots[slot_num] = new_slot
140	                data_dict[CONF_SLOTS] = new_slots

    @callback
    def _request_sync_check_if_relevant(
        self, event: Event[EventStateChangedData]
    ) -> None:
        """
        Request sync check only if the state change is for a tracked entity.

        Used by the catch-all state tracking fallback to avoid unnecessary
        checks on every HA state change. Falls back to always-checking if
        entity IDs haven't been discovered yet.
        """
        if not self._tracked_entity_ids or (
            event.data["entity_id"] in self._tracked_entity_ids
        ):
            self.request_sync_check()

    async def _async_tick(self, _now: datetime | None = None) -> None:
        """Periodic reconciliation tick."""
        if not self._started:
            return

        # sees this tick in ``_tick_tasks``.
        task = asyncio.current_task()
        if task is None:
            return
        self._tick_tasks.add(task)
        try:
            # Try upgrading before the state check — catch-all mode may prevent
            # request_sync_check from firing for entities not yet tracked
            self._try_upgrade_state_tracking()

            if self._state in (
                SyncState.IN_SYNC,
                SyncState.SYNCING,
                SyncState.SUSPENDED,
            ):
                return

            await self._async_tick_impl()
        finally:
            self._tick_tasks.discard(task)

        """

            return

            if slot_state.active_state not in (STATE_ON, STATE_OFF):

content.content:
# A pending write only gates reconciliation while it still reflects
            # the desired state. If the user changed the PIN or disabled the slot
            # while the write was outstanding, the entry is stale: drop it and
            # reconcile the new target instead of holding PENDING_CONFIRMATION
            # (and re-syncing the old value) until the deadline.
            still_wanted = (
                slot_state.active_state == STATE_ON
                and slot_state.pin_state == believed_pin
            )
            if still_wanted and time.monotonic() < deadline:
                if self._state is not SyncState.PENDING_CONFIRMATION:
                    self._state = SyncState.PENDING_CONFIRMATION
                    self._write_state()
                return
            # Drop the entry and re-sync on the NEXT tick. Returning here (rather
            # than falling through to _perform_sync this tick) lets a confirming
            # push that lands between ticks resolve the slot first, and avoids
            # double-charging the breaker when the re-sync itself also fails.
            del self._lock._pending_writes[self._slot_num]
            if still_wanted:
                # Genuine timeout: the write we still want never confirmed.
                self._slot_breaker.record_failure()
                _LOGGER.warning(
                    "%s: optimistic write not confirmed within the timeout; "
                    "re-syncing (attempt %s)",
                    self._log_prefix,
                    self._slot_breaker.failure_count,
                )
            # A stale entry (desired state changed) is not a sync failure, so it
            # does not charge the breaker.
            self._state = SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC
            self._write_state()
            return

        # -- OUT_OF_SYNC: check lock reachability, then attempt sync --
        if self._coordinator.unreachable:
            self._state = SyncState.SUSPENDED
            self._write_state()
            return

        if expected_in_sync:
            # Became in sync without us doing anything (external change)
            self._state = SyncState.IN_SYNC
            self._slot_breaker.reset()
            self._write_state()
            self._clear_resolved_issues(slot_state)
            return

        # Circuit breaker check: too many failed sync attempts (a set that
        # never converges, or repeated LockOperationFailed) suspends the slot.
        if self._slot_breaker.tripped:
            _LOGGER.error(
                "%s: Sync attempts exceeded (%s in %s window), suspending slot",
                self._log_prefix,
                self._slot_breaker.failure_count,
                SYNC_ATTEMPT_WINDOW,
            )
            self._suspend_slot(
                slot_state,
                f"Lock **{self._lock.lock.entity_id}**: slot "
                f"**{self._slot_num}** failed to sync after "
                f"{self._slot_breaker.failure_count} consecutive attempts. "
                f"The lock may be rejecting the code silently or "
                f"experiencing communication issues. "
                f"Sync has been suspended for this slot. It will resume "
                f"automatically once the lock accepts the code or you change "
                f"the PIN for this slot.",
            )
            return

        # Perform sync
        self._state = SyncState.SYNCING
        self._write_state()
        was_set = False
        try:
            was_set = await self._perform_sync(slot_state)
        except CodeRejectedError as err:
            _LOGGER.error("%s: Code rejected: %s", self._log_prefix, err)
            await self._disable_slot(
                f"Lock **{err.lock_entity_id}**: slot **{err.code_slot}** "
                f"has been disabled. {err}\n\n"
                f"Fix the issue and re-enable the slot.",
            )
            # After disable, the slot active switch turns off. The next
            # request_sync_check will see the slot as in-sync (no code
            # desired, no code on lock). Set to OUT_OF_SYNC so the next
            # tick resolves to IN_SYNC.
            self._state = SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC
            return
        except LockDisconnected as err:
            _LOGGER.info(
                "%s: Lock unreachable during %s usercode: %s. Will retry on next tick.",
                self._log_prefix,
                "set" if slot_state.active_state == STATE_ON else "clear",
                err,
            )
            # Connectivity failure: feed the lock breaker so repeated failures
            # converge to "unreachable" alongside poll failures (recovers via a
            # successful poll/push).
            self._coordinator.note_connectivity_failure()
            self._state = SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC
            return
        except LockOperationFailed as err:
            _LOGGER.info(
                "%s: Operation failed during %s usercode: %s. Will retry on next tick.",
                self._log_prefix,
                "set" if slot_state.active_state == STATE_ON else "clear",
                err,
            )
            # The lock is reachable but the operation failed. Count toward the
            # slot breaker so a persistently-failing slot suspends instead of
            # retrying forever -- NOT the lock breaker, whose read-probe
            # recovery can't validate a failing write.
            self._slot_breaker.record_failure()
            self._state = SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC
            return
        except Exception as err:
            _LOGGER.exception(
                "%s: Unexpected error during %s usercode. "
                "Sync suspended for this lock to prevent infinite retry loop. "
                "Error: %s: %s",
                self._log_prefix,
                "set" if slot_state.active_state == STATE_ON else "clear",
                type(err).__name__,
                err,
            )
            self._suspend_slot(
                slot_state,
                f"Lock **{self._lock.lock.entity_id}**: slot **{self._slot_num}** "
                f"encountered an unexpected error during sync. This may indicate a bug "
                f"in the lock code manager integration. Check logs for details and "
                f"report this issue.\n\nError: {type(err).__name__}: {err}",
            )
            return
        else:
            # Sync succeeded — refresh coordinator to verify.
            # Skip for push providers — they update coordinator optimistically
            # via push_update() and refreshing from cache could read stale data.
            if not self._lock.supports_push:
                try:
                    await self._coordinator.async_refresh()
                except Exception:
                    _LOGGER.exception(
                        "%s: Coordinator refresh failed after sync operation. "
                        "Sync may have succeeded but verification failed. "
                        "Will retry on next tick.",
                        self._log_prefix,
                    )
                    # Treat an unverified set the same as a verification
                    # miss: repeated unverified sets must eventually trip
                    # the slot breaker, otherwise a persistently failing

content.content:
def _write_state(self) -> None:
        """Notify the entity to write Home Assistant state."""
        # Skip if stopped: a tick mid-await may still call _write_state after
        # async_stop has begun teardown of the owning entity.
        if not self._started:
            return
        self._state_writer(self.in_sync)

    @callback
    def request_sync_check(self, *_args: Any) -> None:
        """
        Request a sync check on the next tick.

        Transitions IN_SYNC -> OUT_OF_SYNC if calculate_in_sync returns False
        (also requests a breaker reset since the sync target changed).
        Transitions SUSPENDED -> OUT_OF_SYNC if the coordinator is no longer
        suspended. No-op for LOADING, OUT_OF_SYNC, SYNCING.

        The breaker is not mutated here -- this callback can fire while a
        tick is awaiting ``_perform_sync``, and a direct ``reset()`` could
        clear failure state that the tick is about to read. Instead we set
        ``_breaker_reset_requested`` and the next tick consumes it.
        """
        if self._state is SyncState.IN_SYNC:
            slot_state = self._resolve_slot_state()
            if slot_state is not None and not self.calculate_in_sync(slot_state):
                self._state = SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC
                self._breaker_reset_requested = True
                self._write_state()
        elif self._state is SyncState.SUSPENDED:
            if self._code_suspend_target is not None:
                # Suspended for a non-converging code or an unexpected error.
                # Only retry once the desired target changes (e.g. the user
                # edits the PIN or toggles the slot) or the slot returns to
                # sync on its own -- otherwise stay suspended so we don't
                # hammer the lock with a code it keeps rejecting.
                slot_state = self._resolve_slot_state()
                if slot_state is not None and (
                    (slot_state.active_state, slot_state.pin_state)
                    != self._code_suspend_target
                    or self.calculate_in_sync(slot_state)
                ):
                    self._breaker_reset_requested = True
                    self._code_suspend_target = None
                    self._state = SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC
                    self._write_state()
            elif not self._coordinator.unreachable:
                # Suspended because the lock was unreachable; it is reachable
                # again, so resume.
                self._breaker_reset_requested = True
                self._state = SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC
                self._write_state()

    @callback
    def _request_sync_check_if_relevant(
        self, event: Event[EventStateChangedData]

async def test_syncing_to_out_of_sync_on_lock_disconnected(
        self,
        hass: HomeAssistant,
        mock_lock_config_entry,
        lock_code_manager_config_entry,
    ) -> None:
        """SYNCING transitions to OUT_OF_SYNC on LockDisconnected."""
        entity_obj = get_in_sync_entity_obj(hass, SLOT_1_IN_SYNC_ENTITY)
        manager = entity_obj._sync_manager

        manager._state = SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC
        manager._coordinator.data[1] = SlotCredential.empty()

        with patch.object(
            manager,
            "_perform_sync",
            new_callable=AsyncMock,
            side_effect=LockDisconnected("test"),
        ):
            await manager._async_tick()
            await hass.async_block_till_done()

        assert manager._state is SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC

    async def test_syncing_to_suspended_on_unexpected_error(
        self,
        hass: HomeAssistant,
        mock_lock_config_entry,
        lock_code_manager_config_entry,
    ) -> None:
        """SYNCING transitions to SUSPENDED on generic exception."""
        entity_obj = get_in_sync_entity_obj(hass, SLOT_1_IN_SYNC_ENTITY)
        manager = entity_obj._sync_manager

        manager._state = SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC
        manager._coordinator.data[1] = SlotCredential.empty()

        with patch.object(
            manager,
            "_perform_sync",
            new_callable=AsyncMock,
            side_effect=RuntimeError("unexpected"),
        ):
            await manager._async_tick()
            await hass.async_block_till_done()

        assert manager._state is SyncState.SUSPENDED
        assert manager._coordinator.unreachable is False

    async def test_syncing_to_suspended_on_circuit_breaker(

async def test_tick_suspends_while_provider_setup_not_validated(
        self,
        hass: HomeAssistant,
        mock_lock_config_entry,
        lock_code_manager_config_entry,
    ) -> None:
        """A lock whose provider setup has not validated suspends sync quietly.

        A structurally-degraded lock (e.g. zero usable PIN slots after a
        re-inclusion) is kept in runtime data with entities, but attempting
        writes against it would fail on the capability probe and land in the
        generic error handler — raising a misleading "report this bug"
        suspension repair on top of the accurate lock_setup_failed one, and
        re-firing the recovery device query on every attempt. The tick must
        suspend without calling _perform_sync, and resume via
        request_sync_check once validation succeeds.
        """
        entity_obj = get_in_sync_entity_obj(hass, SLOT_1_IN_SYNC_ENTITY)
        manager = entity_obj._sync_manager

        manager._state = SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC
        manager._coordinator.data[1] = SlotCredential.empty()
        manager._lock._setup_succeeded = False

        with patch.object(
            manager, "_perform_sync", new_callable=AsyncMock
        ) as perform_sync:
            await manager._async_tick()
            await hass.async_block_till_done()

        perform_sync.assert_not_called()
        assert manager._state is SyncState.SUSPENDED

        # Validation succeeds later (re-interview + integration reload); the
        # next sync-check request resumes reconciliation.
        manager._lock._setup_succeeded = True
        manager.request_sync_check()
        assert manager._state is SyncState.OUT_OF_SYNC

    async def test_suspended_does_not_resume_while_setup_not_validated(
        self,
        hass: HomeAssistant,
        mock_lock_config_entry,
        lock_code_manager_config_entry,
    ) -> None:
        """request_sync_check keeps an unvalidated lock suspended even when reachable."""
        entity_obj = get_in_sync_entity_obj(hass, SLOT_1_IN_SYNC_ENTITY)
        manager = entity_obj._sync_manager

        manager._state = SyncState.SUSPENDED
        manager._code_suspend_target = None
        manager._lock._setup_succeeded = False
        assert manager._coordinator.unreachable is False

        manager.request_sync_check()
        assert manager._state is SyncState.SUSPENDED
